title: Quadlet - single file specification
body:
### Feature request description

For a multi-container, multi-volume service, there's no easy way to capture the specification in a single place -- I've to create separate container files, pod files, network files, volume files etc. There's kube files but they also cannot contain everything and have their own specification. This makes maintenance as well as sharing an installation spec for any project a complete headache  because there's no single file to capture all the information about a service. Further, it's especially jarring when migrating to quadlet from docker compose, which is the most widepread format.

Since quadlets _generate_ the corresponding unit files, it can very well digest a single file that contains the whole spec for a particular service with all its parts --  maybe in the pod file itself, or a new .quad format or similar. 

### Suggest potential solution

Ideal solution - a new file format which is 1 single file with all spec associated with the service -- it's volumes, containers, networks all in 1 place.  It can be a brand-new file format with YAML like indented file-format; or it can be the pod file itself, with all associated container, volume, network files concatenated, and each sub-section (eg:`[Container]`) specifying that part.
Either of these work, but quadlets need to improve the input specification in a way that's easier to share, deploy, maintain.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
The reason we have multiple files is because each file corresponds to one systemd service.

Changing that makes the design much more problematic IMO as you loose that one to one mapping. And defining yet another newly invented syntax is not going to improve the situation as you now invented several competing quadlet formats which makes everything much more confusing and harder to maintain.

I also don't really buy that a single file makes it easier to deploy. The vast majority of applications will require additional data such as config files or some shared state. So in many cases just copying a single file is not good enough anyway.


So overall I am strictly against yet another file format but I leave it open in case other maintainers think otherwise.

> several competing quadlet formats which makes everything much more confusing and harder to maintain.

It doesn't have to be a different format -- it can be the exact same specification for `[Volume]`, `[Container]`, `[Network]` just allow them to be specified in the same file, with some sort of a de-limiter for this purpose. And since it's a quadlet translation layer, nothing necessitates it to be a 1 to 1 mapping.

 > I also don't really buy that a single file makes it easier to deploy.

I think you're underestimating the value of this. Yes, most projects have their own configuration files, but all of them start with "download this docker compose" or copy below code blob for docker compose; and then create a conf which has good defaults OR just use the web GUI for all the configuration. They don't describe the install set up as "copy these N files" or clone this git repo containing all these files and install them into systemd directory one by one.
The very reason docker compose is as successful as it is, is due to the ease with which it's setup can be passed on from one user to another. And I think podman needs a similarly portable and powerful mechanism to represent full set up.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@apparle how would you design the translations of the dependencies between different units?
For example:
```
// a.volume
[Volume]
VolumeName=bar

// b.volume
[Volume]

// app.container
[Container]
Image=foo
Volume=a.volume:/a
Volume=b.volume:/b
```

With the current implementation, Quadlet first handles the `.volume` files. Then, when it handles the `.container` file it knows to translate `Volume=a.volume:/a` to `-v bar:/a` and `Volume=b.volume:/b` to `-v systemd-b:/b` and also to add `Requires=` and `After=` for `a-volume.service` and `b-volume.service`. 
So the generated `app.service` will be:
```
---app.service---
[X-Container]
Image=foo
Volume=a.volume:/a
Volume=b.volume:/b

[Unit]
Wants=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
After=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
SourcePath=/tmp/quadlet-volumes/app.container
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers
Requires=a-volume.service
After=a-volume.service
Requires=b-volume.service
After=b-volume.service

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
KillMode=mixed
ExecStop=/usr/local/bin/podman rm -v -f -i systemd-%N
ExecStopPost=-/usr/local/bin/podman rm -v -f -i systemd-%N
Delegate=yes
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all
SyslogIdentifier=%N
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/podman run --name systemd-%N --replace --rm --cgroups=split --sdnotify=conmon -d -v bar:/a -v systemd-b:/b foo
```

I was imagining this as a 2-step translation. So, in your example above, let's say the first file is called myapp.quadlet (containing volumes, containers, networks inside them; and let's say there's many such quadlet files for many apps that share networks etc. First step will just convert them into separate .container, .volume, .network files maybe just in memory or tmp directory etc. And then next step will process volume, network, container files as usual, propagating network and volume information in the right order.

> First step will just convert them into separate .container, .volume, .network files maybe just in memory or tmp directory etc.

But there are no file names that can be used from there.

This is an anti feature and should not be done at all. Having two different ways of doing things will cause nothing but confusion so I am very strongly against this and vote to just close this here.

> vote to just close this here

Fine by me

> But there are no file names that can be used from there.

You can have the file names be defined as part of the syntax. Say ` --- a.volume ---` or something along the lines of `// a.volume` as you described earlier:

```
--- a.volume ---
[Volume]
VolumeName=bar

--- b.volume ---
[Volume]

--- app.container ---
[Container]
Image=foo
Volume=a.volume:/a
Volume=b.volume:/b
```

> Having two different ways of doing things will cause nothing but confusion so I am very strongly against this and vote to just close this here.

The only reason to suggest a different way to do things is because the existing way is clunky and non-intuitive. If not a simple concat of the files, maybe consider a different & more elegant format; I'm not hung up on a specific way. 
Yes, that's a subjective opinion, but as someone who's migrated from compose files to quadlet files and using them for quite some time now, I all the more appreciate the brevity and elegance of a compose files.
Having said that, if you guys as maintainers strongly feel that existing specification format is great as is, I'll not drag this issue. It can be closed.

I wonder if this doesn't make more sense as a `podman quadlet install`  feature - single file gets provided, gets broken up at install time, we end up with N individual quadlets (so at runtime, everything is the same). We would need to make sure that the entire collection can be removed together as well, so we can easily redeploy, but as a convenience feature to deploy a bunch of units at once, single-file multiple units makes sense to me.

> single file gets provided, gets broken up at install time, we end up with N individual quadlets (so at runtime, everything is the same)

How would this be easier then? We still have to maintain a separate parser then. How would you know how to name the new files? And then we have two ways to achieve the same result which I think is simply worse then having one file per service. That is how systemd works after all.
`podman quadlet install` can already use a directly or install multiple remote files? Would it then not be easier to point it at a remote git directory then with multiple files? I really don't see the point that having two competing ways of doing this will do us any favours. And if you lock that format to podman quadlet install it would certainly be even more confusing if a user cannot even copy the file directly into the right place.

I think the attraction here is in adding the convenience of `docker compose` to Quadlets. Listing off 5 URLs or filepaths to `podman quadlet install` is a lot less convenient than just one. Installing from a directory does work, but it still can't match the `docker compose` model - "I copied some YAML off a webpage into a file, did a `docker compose up`, and everything just worked". I don't think we're really close to that right now, and I fully understand the desire to get us closer.

At the same time, we really don't want to change Quadlet runtime behavior; preserving a 1:1 of Quadlet file to systemd unit makes a lot of sense.

I'm trying to bridge these in a way that still makes sense while preserving ~most of the convenience of the `docker compose` model.

> At the same time, we really don't want to change Quadlet runtime behavior; preserving a 1:1 of Quadlet file to systemd unit makes a lot of sense.
> I'm trying to bridge these in a way that still makes sense while preserving ~most of the convenience of the docker compose model.

By special casing parsing in podman quadlet install we still have to maintain the exact same thing so we gain but users then loose the ability to actually deploy such a quadlet without running that command which IMO would the worst possible outcome of that. Because then you actually break the ability to use that single file format for everyone doing their stuff in declarative way actually just copying the file to their server.

I really really do not want to maintain two competing file formats on how to write quadlets in this repo. That fact simply makes no sense to me.

> "I copied some YAML off a webpage into a file, did a docker compose up, and everything just worked". I don't think we're really close to that right now, and I fully understand the desire to get us closer.

Well they can use compose already against podman if they want to use compose files. There is already tooling to convert that automatically into quadlets (https://github.com/containers/podlet) if that is wanted even.

Sure I get that that projects document copy my compose and edit it today but there is no reason that the same must apply to quadlet. Any upstream project providing support for quadlet files would simply have them committed as multiple files. Does that mean downloading multiple files, sure? But do we actually improve this at all by having a second file format all of the sudden? You just end up with some projects using one and some the other? And like I said before many applications already are not single file today as they come with config files and such.
So what really should needs fixing is the ability to download multiple files and thus my previous suggestion to support cloning a git repo directory just like podman build already can.

> There is already tooling to convert that automatically into quadlets (https://github.com/containers/podlet) if that is wanted even.

I could never get this to work. I had better success by asking LLM chatbots to convert my compose files to quadlet files instead. 

@mheon 
> I wonder if this doesn't make more sense as a podman quadlet install feature - single file gets provided, gets broken up at install time, we end up with N individual quadlets (so at runtime, everything is the same).

Isn't quadlet files being translated from `.container`/`.network`/`.volume` files to the systemd `.service` file effectively the install step? Is another step really a good idea?
Usually most container specs needs to be maintained/updated periodically (eg: for image version updates, slight tweaks to commands etc.) and as a user I definitely don't want to _install_ one file-format and then maintain another for any changes. 
So now, if user is only maintaining the new file format and we ask user to rerun `podman quadlet install` after any change, cleaning up old files will be messy. On top of this, user must do `systemd daemon-reload` to translate the quadlet files again.
Instead `systemd daemon-reload` itself being the combined step that does all intermediate translation and removes all old files is a more intuitive. 

@Luap99 
> I really really do not want to maintain two competing file formats on how to write quadlets in this repo. 

This I completely understand. That's why I wasn't proposing a brand new format but instead just a plain concatenation, with file names (eg: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26447#issuecomment-3106620130), and literally nothing else changed. It's similar to how many linux /etc conf files that have a `xxxx.conf` and `xxxx.conf.d` variants. Both refer to exact same documentation, usage behavior etc. And it's a matter of user convenience on whether they want to write small files or a single big file. 
Even in code I'm assuming nothing else changes except 1 extra intermediate step to split files; all other parsers / translators stay the same. That way no separate code base, extra man pages, extra documentation etc.

> preserving a 1:1 of Quadlet file to systemd unit makes a lot of sense.

Pardon my naivety but why is this so important? 
Let's say we invent a new format (eg: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26447#issuecomment-3106620130) and then that's used to derive intermediate quadlet files. And then from the intermediately derived quadlet file, there's a 1:1 quadlet file to systemd unit file mapping maintained -- why is that not good enough?
What's the downside to having a 1 source file ==> many unit files ?

Alternative world-view: 
It's not just about 1-file vs. N-files but a general need for a more elegant & intuitive file-format (https://xkcd.com/927/ 😄 ). And for that purpose I totally understand that _this repo_ probably isn't the right place to do so.
Instead, someone who's unhappy enough / motivated enough, should invent a brand new composite file format along with a corresponding systemd generator in a new repo. And that generator directly creates systemd service files using `podman run` and other podman CLI tools, bypassing quadlets altogether. At least from what I can understand quadlet is just a systemd generator and in theory a different generator can pretty much do everything quadlets can with different file formats.
And then how much traction it gains in community -- that'll be a better metric of whether it is a good idea or not.

But I'm not going that far yet; instead I'm just proposing to create something far more analogous to `xxxx.conf` & `xxxx.conf.d`.

Just to present the idea further, more concretely. I created an example implementation - [multiquadlet-gen-example.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21529909/multiquadlet-gen-example.tar.gz) - that does the file splitting as separate systemd generator and then calls quadlet with input directories hijacked with `QUADLET_UNIT_DIRS`. I've attached my script as well as 2 example multi-container apps that work quite well with this generator on my machine.

Note that this is just an example prototype and not a solution -- it doesn't allow mixing of references across standalone `.network` or `.volume` files from inside inside my `.multiquadlet` files because of separate generator calls and potential systemd race. But a real implementation that's part of podman's quadlet generator codebase and not hijacking the `QUADLET_UNIT_DIRS` variable would work seamlessly.

@Luap99 @ygalblum hopefully that clarifies what I had in mind concretely, and why I think it's not too disruptive.

PS: Mistakenly hit the wrong button to close issue, so reopened it. Sorry for the spam.

> just means confusion as it is not clear which one should use. 

I wasn't suggesting one over the other, but supporting both and letting a user decide -- very much like `xxxx.conf` vs`xxxx.conf.d`. But fair enough if you don't see that as value but unnecessary complexity, I won't belabor this any further.
The github discussion exists if others want to debate the merits of 1 vs. many files and attempt to convince you otherwise 😄.


> Yes that is is right. And I understand the ask for a generator from compose to service files, that to me makes sense because compose files exists already and are a well known format. Adding a new compose like format on the other hand would not make sense to me. That does not mean I would like to maintain or support such a thing but I think reusing an existing file format that fits the purpose will always be objectively better than creating another competing file format.

While the compose spec is decent for that app, it isn't systemd first and lacks a bunch of things compared to quadlets:
- Ability to load podman secrets as env variables into containers instead of files. 
- Systemd integration and ability to interact / depend / order with other systemd services.
- Using Type=notify and explicitly notification inside the container as opposed to just Healthcheck

So just that spec as-is isn't good enough to implement IMO. Maybe a derivative format with extra addons, but then that's back to the xkcd comic and one more competing format ¯\_(ツ)_/¯ .
The current podman-compose repo doesn't attempt to use systemd generation + separate services per container/network/volume at all, but instead just a flat service with whole file so that's completely orthogonal.
Maybe I'll explore the direction of compose based service generators in a separate repo down the road.

Sorry for not responding before. Seeing @Luap99 objection I didn't want to push too much from my end.

On one hand, I understand the use case and see the appeal. But, I also see the pandora box that comes with this feature. The naive case is always simple, but then comes the questions on how to support instances and drop-ins, how to handle processing order, etc.

In summary, I don't feel as strong as either of you do. So, I'm leaving it to you to make a decision. I don't mind reviewing the code if you decide to go with it

Over the weekend, I decided to take the idea all the way -- cleaned up that prototype code, ported to python, posted it on github with examples: https://github.com/apparle/multiquadlet . And also ported my servers to use this with a variety of complex container setups and it's working well.

This can sit outside the podman tree and still reuse all of podman's quadlet generator features by leveraging the env variable `QUADLET_UNIT_DIRS`. I know it's not an official supported env variable only intended for internal testing use; but as long as that env variable isn't broken, it should just work even with new podman release & features.

Now people can try the idea to see if they like it. Let's see if it gains traction in the community -- if yes, we can revisit integrating the concept into podman codebase. If no, then I've a solution for my personal use which I can maintain and use myself.

I'm okay if you guys want to close the issue for now.

kube yaml file + kube quadlet is the most simple way close to your request. I wrote up some helm chart for podman kube play, now I deploy the helm chart rendered kube yaml file + quadlet with Terraform(helm data, remote_file, null resource etc.), I think this is the way that I enjoy work with podman
helm charts for podman
https://github.com/fsdrw08/helm-charts/tree/246660664bc8e9cf7c863a19ad0c71fb93d747c9/charts


example usage:
https://github.com/fsdrw08/SoloLab/tree/main/TerraformWorkShop/Quadlet/zot
This will be in 6.0
